steps.title: Identify the signal
steps.content: The narrator describes Sir Winston Day's appearance as suited for a bust or stamp and his forehead as “evidently bulging with grey matter.” But then comes a sarcastic twist: it would be “impertinent to inquire too closely into the actual achievements his half century of public service had produced,” and his memoirs only get to relevant details after thirty pages — details the narrator implies no one reads.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'He has the appearance of a distinguished figure, but it is uncertain whether he has accomplished anything to earn distinction.': the text establishes a distinguished appearance (bust, stamp, bulging forehead) while sarcasm casts doubt on real achievements — a direct match. ✓
  • Choice B — 'He looks like a person worthy of respect, but his memoirs reveal that some of his actions were dishonorable.': the text says nothing about dishonorable actions; the mystery is whether he accomplished anything, not whether he behaved badly. ✗
  • Choice C — 'He would be a celebrated public figure if his achievements did not have to be kept secret.': the “state secrets” line is a joke about reader indifference, not a sincere claim about classified accomplishments. ✗
  • Choice D — 'He has maintained a modest profile even though he has served the public capably for many years.': the text associates him with an impressive appearance, not modesty, and treats his accomplishments as an unresolved enigma rather than established capability. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
